front: What is the formal definition of a compact set in a metric space?
back: A subset K of a metric space (X, d) is compact if every open cover of K has a finite subcover. That is, for every collection of open sets whose union contains K, there exists a finite number of these sets whose union still contains K.

front: State the Finite Intersection Property (FIP) and its connection to compactness.
back: A collection of sets has the Finite Intersection Property if every finite subcollection has a non-empty intersection. A metric space is compact if and only if for every collection of closed sets with the FIP, the entire collection has a non-empty intersection.

front: What is a crucial consequence of compactness regarding sequences?
back: In a compact metric space, every sequence has a convergent subsequence. This is known as sequential compactness, and in metric spaces, it is equivalent to the topological definition of compactness (open cover compactness).

front: How are compactness and completeness related in metric spaces?
back: Every compact metric space is complete. The converse is not true; a complete metric space is not necessarily compact (e.g., the real numbers R with the standard metric is complete but not compact).

front: What does it mean for a subset of Rⁿ to be compact (by the Heine-Borel Theorem)?
back: A subset K of Rⁿ (with the standard Euclidean metric) is compact if and only if it is both closed and bounded.
